part: When There Was No Such Thing
title: He Ran Up Two Flights to Enroll
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Midtown Manhattan in July 2011, Jack Greenberg recalls starting at Columbia Law School with no thought of civil rights work, since the label did not yet exist, until he saw Walter Gellhorn's seminar called Legal Survey on the bulletin board and ran up two flights of stairs to enroll in what amounted to an early form of clinical legal education on real cases.

part: When There Was No Such Thing
title: A Course Named to Hide From the Faculty
summary: Greenberg explains that Gellhorn called it Legal Survey because naming it Civil Rights risked the charge of being a Communist, a charge that twice summoned Gellhorn before Congress, and recounts taking the course four semesters in a country with only a handful of civil rights lawyers, until the departure of LDF attorney Marian Wynne Perry opened a slot he was recommended to fill.

part: From the Courtroom to the Country
title: Thurgood Marshall's Small Band
summary: Greenberg describes the tiny Legal Defense and Educational Fund he joined in 1949 under an already nationally known Thurgood Marshall, fresh from the restrictive covenant and voting rights cases, alongside first assistant Robert L. Carter, Franklin H. Williams (later ambassador to Ghana), a full-time staff sociologist, and himself, a unit that spun off from the NAACP over tax exemption in 1957.

part: From the Courtroom to the Country
title: A Cosmic Decision
summary: Greenberg recounts trying the Topeka, Kansas school case with Robert L. Carter on two tracks, the measurable inequality of black schools and the argument that segregation itself is inequality, and the stunned silence when Brown v. Board of Education came down in May 1954, a cosmic decision that swept away all state-imposed segregation and provoked vehement Southern resistance because schools meant mass numbers and the taboo of mixing the sexes.

part: From the Courtroom to the Country
title: Thirty-Five Hundred Acquitted
summary: Becoming director-counsel in 1961 as the sit-in movement spread like wildfire and students were arrested across the South, Greenberg recalls that there were no ready defenses because no one had needed them before, and that the LDF, armed with constitutional arguments, ended up representing some thirty-five hundred people and getting them all acquitted.

part: From the Courtroom to the Country
title: A Pro and Anti Review
summary: Greenberg describes writing Race Relations in American Law, published in 1959 as the first book on the subject since about 1919, and its curious reception: the Columbia Law Review ran both a pro and an anti review as if the field were controversial, set against Herbert Wechsler's Toward Neutral Principles and Edmond Cahn's attack on Brown for its use of social science, while a reviewer he approached begged off because he was white and married to a black woman.

part: The New Laws and the School Wars
title: An Ice Cream Cone at the Airport
summary: Greenberg recalls bringing cases to desegregate segregated airport terminals, including one in Atlanta and one in New Orleans where co-counsel A.P. Tureaud, long the only black lawyer in the city, brought his granddaughter to buy an ice cream cone she had never been allowed before, and the 1963 program he launched giving black lawyers ten thousand dollars to open practices where there were none, seeding figures like Julius Chambers, Mel Watt, and Marian Wright Edelman.

part: The New Laws and the School Wars
title: The Market Did Not Take Care of It
summary: As the Civil Rights Act of 1964 took shape after Kennedy's bill and assassination, Greenberg describes crafting a litigation program: public accommodations proved easy since few owners truly resisted, hotels dodged the law with private clubs handing white guests membership cards, and Title VII employment brought the bitterest fights against labor unions that were pro civil rights nationally but kept segregated white and black locals.

title: My Left Flank Is in Retreat, I Attack
summary: Greenberg traces the school fight into the late 1960s, the real estate stakes and Nixon's Southern Strategy of resisting integration for political advantage, and the maneuver in Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education when Senator John C. Stennis held up the armed services appropriation and HEW Secretary Robert Finch asked the court of appeals not to decide, forcing the LDF to reach the Supreme Court through the ancient common-law writ of certiorari, a gambit Greenberg likened to Marshal Foch's line at the Marne.
related_events: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education

title: There He Stood Naked
summary: Greenberg recounts Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education, where the outcome was uncertain and a preliminary vote favored upholding the lower court, until Warren Burger found himself outvoted in conference and, as retired Chief Justice Earl Warren told Greenberg, stood naked, so Burger switched his vote to control the opinion, assigned it to himself, and wrote an opinion calling for quotas, busing, and integrated teachers, while burying a last paragraph warning that one day this would have to end.
related_events: Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education

title: Fifty Yards Across the Line
summary: Greenberg assesses Milliken v. Bradley in 1974 and San Antonio v. Rodriguez, judging Milliken significant for drawing the line at the political boundary between city and suburb, so that two families living fifty yards apart fell under separate school systems, which he calls a political decision rather than a worked-out legal one, before the recording breaks off.
